items.q: If you double your distance from a point source of waves, what happens to the intensity?
items.correct: It falls to one quarter — intensity follows an inverse-square law with distance from a point source.
items.misconception: It halves — intensity is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged because the wave doesn't lose energy.
items.why: Students default to linear scaling instead of the inverse-square law that arises because the same energy spreads over a sphere of growing surface area.

items.q: Why does Faraday's law include a minus sign (Lenz's law)?
items.correct: The induced current flows in a direction that opposes the change in flux that produced it — a consequence of energy conservation.
items.misconception: The minus sign is a mathematical convention with no physical meaning.
items.random_wrong: The minus sign means the current flows backwards through the wire.
items.why: Students treat the minus sign as a notational quirk rather than encoding Lenz's law, the deep statement that induced effects oppose their cause.

items.q: Do all stars end their lives as supernovae?
items.correct: No — only sufficiently massive stars (above about 8 solar masses) explode as supernovae; lower-mass stars become white dwarfs.
items.random_wrong: Yes — but only after billions of years on Earth's scale.
items.why: Students learn 'star death' as one storyline and don't separate the different end states based on initial mass.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: It's 5 km. “7 km” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: Correct answer: Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
